security_sensitivity.notes: No credentials, PII, or network exposure in current scripts. Future MCP server must enforce allowed-roots isolation and refuse writes outside configured roots.

performance_sensitivity.notes: Docs-lint and gardener scripts run on docs trees, expected to be fast. Future MCP code-search index may have indexing latency for large repos.

design_sensitivity.notes: No UI surface; CLI and MCP tool outputs are plain text or JSON.

design_evidence.stack_specific_source: No UI roots found; CLI-only tooling and seed-prompt text files

persona_candidates.name: framework-operator
persona_candidates.evidence: The install seed (010) and upgrade seed (160) describe a distinct operator role that installs Wavefoundry into a target repository, runs lifecycle commands, reviews diffs, and controls git commits. This operator has a specific workflow and failure mode set (incorrect epoch, stale prompt surface, drift between seed source and rendered docs).

persona_candidates.name: wave-coordinator
persona_candidates.evidence: The wave-coordinator persona is described extensively across seeds (010, 050, 100, 110, 180) with specific operating identity, salience triggers, and responsibilities for managing wave execution, admission, reviewer allocation, and closure in a target repository. The framework ships a wave-coordinator agent doc template.

01.name: Codebase
01.rationale: Single repository with a single deployable target (framework zip). No multi-deploy or per-branch environment pattern yet.

02.name: Dependencies
02.rationale: Python scripts currently use only stdlib. Future MCP package (pyproject.toml) will introduce declared dependencies requiring pinning and isolation. Becomes applicable once pyproject.toml is added.

03.name: Config
03.rationale: Framework reads docs/workflow-config.json for lifecycle epoch and wave settings. Future MCP server reads allowed_roots config. Config values differ meaningfully by installation.

04.name: Backing services
04.rationale: No external databases, caches, or APIs used. Future MCP server is local-only.

05.name: Build / release / run
05.rationale: build_pack.py produces a dated zip distribution with VERSION stamping. Strict build/release semantics (letter suffix, date stamping) are already implemented.

06.name: Processes
06.rationale: Framework scripts are single-invocation CLIs. Future MCP server may have a process model but is not yet designed.

07.name: Port binding
07.rationale: Future MCP server may bind a port or use stdio. Not yet implemented. Re-evaluate when server is scaffolded.

08.name: Concurrency
08.rationale: No concurrent scaling requirement. Scripts are single-process. Future MCP server will handle one request at a time (stdio transport).

09.name: Disposability
09.rationale: Future MCP server as a long-running daemon will need graceful startup/shutdown. Not yet implemented. Re-evaluate when server is scaffolded.

10.name: Dev / prod parity
10.rationale: No distinct dev/staging/prod environments. All operation is local.

11.name: Logs
11.rationale: Framework scripts print to stdout/stderr directly. No structured logging system. Future MCP server may warrant structured logs.

12.name: Admin processes
12.rationale: lifecycle_id.py, build_pack.py, render_platform_surfaces.py, docs_lint.py, and docs_gardener.py are all admin/operational CLI commands. These are the primary interface for maintainers.

13.name: API first
13.rationale: The future MCP tool surface (wave.current, wave.validate, code.search, code.read, etc.) is a first-class integration contract. Tool contracts are defined before implementation. This is the primary consumer interface.

14.name: Telemetry
14.rationale: No monitoring, alerting, or tracing requirements. Local tool; no operational dashboard.

15.name: Auth and security
15.rationale: No authentication or multi-user access control. Allowed-roots isolation in the future MCP server is a safety guardrail, not an auth system.
